summary: Microsoft documents DisableEnhancedNotifications on two Defender policy paths. In a clean Win25H2Clean trace, SecurityHealthService.exe read the Security Center Notifications path and consumed DisableEnhancedNotifications = 1.

setting.name: Windows Security Enhanced Notifications
setting.casual_explanation: Windows Security can show non-critical notifications about device health and protection status. This policy hides those extra notifications so only critical ones remain.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change this when they want a quieter Windows Security experience without turning off critical alerts.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Hiding enhanced notifications reduces noise, but it can also hide regular device-health and security reminders.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft publishes DisableEnhancedNotifications on two ADMX-backed policy paths. WindowsDefenderSecurityCenter.admx maps it to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender Security Center\Notifications, and WindowsDefender.admx maps the same value name to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Reporting. In a clean 25H2 VM snapshot, SecurityHealthService.exe read the Security Center Notifications path and consumed DisableEnhancedNotifications = 1 there. When only the Reporting path was set, the Windows Security launch probe still read the Security Center path instead. The same VM already had DisableNotifications = 1 under the Security Center policy branch, so this record stays gated until that broader notification interaction is modeled more cleanly.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Security uses its normal notification behavior for this policy surface.

allowed_values.label: Enhanced notifications allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and Windows Security can continue showing non-critical notifications.

allowed_values.label: Hide non-critical notifications
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Security only shows critical notifications on the documented Security Center policy surface.

targets.notes: This record uses the Security Center Notifications path because the 25H2 VM trace showed SecurityHealthService.exe reading that path directly during a Windows Security launch. The Reporting alias remains a side observation, not the app path.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes DisableEnhancedNotifications = 1 on the Security Center Notifications policy path that the Win25H2Clean Procmon capture showed SecurityHealthService.exe reading.

windows_defaults.label: Windows managed default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows Security systems where the enhanced-notifications policy is not configured

states.rationale: Leave the Security Center enhanced-notifications policy unset.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems where Windows Security notifications should stay at their normal baseline
avoid_for: People intentionally trying to suppress non-critical Windows Security notifications

states.rationale: Keep the policy unset until there is a clear reason to hide non-critical notifications.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows Security to keep its normal notification behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You explicitly want to suppress non-critical Windows Security messages.
tradeoffs: Does not suppress extra device-health and security reminders.

recommended_profiles.label: Hide non-critical Windows Security notifications
avoid_for: Anyone who still wants normal Windows Security reminders

states.rationale: Use the documented Security Center Notifications policy surface that SecurityHealthService.exe queried in the 25H2 VM.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to cut down on non-critical Windows Security notifications and are comfortable with a still-gated policy record.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the most conservative supported baseline or still want routine Windows Security reminders.
tradeoffs: Cuts notification noise on the Security Center policy surface.
The clean VM snapshot already had the broader DisableNotifications sibling policy on the same branch.

evidence.title: WindowsDefenderSecurityCenter.admx enhanced notifications policy
evidence.summary: Local ADMX notes show Notifications_DisableEnhancedNotifications under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender Security Center\Notifications with enabled value 1 and disabled value 0.

evidence.title: WindowsDefender.admx reporting enhanced notifications policy
evidence.summary: Local ADMX notes also show Reporting_DisableEnhancedNotifications under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Reporting with enabled value 1 and disabled value 0.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon baseline for Security Center notifications policy
evidence.summary: On a clean snapshot, SecurityHealthService.exe queried HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender Security Center\Notifications\DisableEnhancedNotifications and got NAME NOT FOUND. The same capture showed DisableNotifications = 1 already present on that branch.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon enabled-state read for Security Center notifications policy
evidence.summary: After writing DisableEnhancedNotifications = 1 on the Security Center Notifications policy path, SecurityHealthService.exe queried the same path and read Data: 1.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon reporting-path alias check
evidence.summary: When only HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Reporting\DisableEnhancedNotifications was set to 1, the Windows Security launch probe still showed SecurityHealthService.exe querying the Security Center Notifications policy path. This capture did not show a matching read from the Reporting path.

evidence.title: Current security provider enhanced notifications write
evidence.summary: The app writes HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender Security Center\Notifications\DisableEnhancedNotifications = 1.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the exact Security Center Notifications value semantics, and the VM Procmon trace shows SecurityHealthService.exe reading DisableEnhancedNotifications from that path. The Reporting alias did not win the runtime probe, and the sibling DisableNotifications baseline is a branch-level side observation rather than a blocker for this exact value.

open_questions: Should the Reporting policy alias be modeled as a separate audit record or folded into this one as a parallel documented path?
Should the app ever write both documented paths, or should it stay on the Security Center path only?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: SecurityHealthService.exe | RegQueryValue | HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender Security Center\Notifications\DisableEnhancedNotifications | SUCCESS | Type: REG_DWORD, Length: 4, Data: 1; baseline run shows the same value as NAME NOT FOUND before the write.
validation_proof.notes: The clean snapshot baseline and the enabled-state VM probe both hit the same Security Center Notifications policy path. A separate alias check with only the Reporting path set still showed SecurityHealthService.exe reading the Security Center path. The baseline also showed DisableNotifications = 1 on that branch, but that sibling policy does not change which path this value is read from.
